revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 海童神社 |画像 = [[画像:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] |所在地 = 長崎県南松浦郡新上五島町有川郷 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} |祭神 = 海童神 |社格 = 旧無格社 |創建 = 不詳 |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = |神事 = }}
'''海童神社'''（かいどうじんじゃ）は、[[長崎県]][[新上五島町]]有川郷船津地区の応護島に鎮座する[[神社]]である。鯨の顎骨を使用した[[鳥居]]があることで有名。
== 祭神 == [[ワタツミ|海童神]]を主祭神に、[[蛭子神]]を[[配祀]]する。
配祀神の蛭子神は、[[合祀]]された船津地区・蛭子神社の祭神である。
== 歴史 == [[ファイル:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|応護島頂上の祠]] [[ファイル:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|鯨の顎骨の鳥居]] 創建年代は不詳。町や[[長崎県]]のほとんどの資料等に記載されている由緒によると、「[[元和 (日本)|元和]]3年（[[1617年]]）～同5年にかけて、有川村では毎年[[6月17日]]に限って海で遊泳する大人・子供に溺死人が相次いだ。時の[[乙名]]役の高井良福右衛門に海童神からの神託によって神子島に石祠を建立し、海童神を奉祀した」と記載されている。また、「翌（元和）6年6月17日には例祭を行い、'''二〇加踊り'''（にわかおどり）と呼ばれる即興の寸劇を奉納したところ、溺死者はなくなった」とされる。その後も二〇加（[[俄]]）の奉納は毎年行われ、現在では有川地区の年中行事である'''十七日祭り'''となっている。
しかし近年では郷土史家による研究が進み、いくつかの矛盾点が指摘されている。[[江戸時代]]中期（[[18世紀]]）から[[明治]]初期（[[19世紀]]後葉）にかけて、村の漁業・農業・風俗等を記した『江口文書』や、[[慶応]]4年（[[1868年]]）に記された[[有川神社|祖母君神社]]の縁起書によると、「[[慶長]]3年（[[1598年]]）の祖母君神社奉遷の際、それを守護する'''応護神'''として有川湾の中ノ島に[[龍神]]を祀り、これに因んで島の名前を'''応護島'''とした」と記述されている。また、当時は「乙名役」という役職もなく、「高井良福右衛門」という人物も[[宝暦]]5年（[[1755年]]）以降に記述があり、この時期には存在していないことが確認された。
[[文久]]4年（[[1864年]]）の資料では「[[宝暦]]2年（[[1752年]]）6月17日に、船乗り等の水難防止を祈願して'''通りもの'''（行列）を始めた」との記述がある。また、全国に伝承される俄の諸記録によると、いずれも江戸中期（18世紀中頃）の「通りもの（練りもの）」から始まり、[[大阪]]・[[京都]]・[[江戸]]での即興笑劇の流行の伝播を受け、江戸時代初頭（19世紀初）より「二〇加（俄）」の芸態が確立されたとされ、有川村が江戸時代最初期（[[17世紀]]初頭）から全国に先駆け「二〇加」を創始していたとは考えにくいため、江戸時代後期以降の創始ではないかとの指摘がされている。
明治中期に船津・蛭子神社を[[合祀]]。同41年（[[1908年]]）に祖母君神社に合祀されている。この時に拝殿や鳥居は破壊され石祠のみ祀られていたが、[[昭和]]2年（[[1927年]]）に鳥居を建立し、同48年（[[1973年]]）に鯨骨の鳥居が日東捕鯨株式会社より奉納されている。 [[平成]]16年（[[2004年]]）には有川港の整備事業によって埋め立てられ陸続きになった。
== 祭祀 == ;主な祭礼・神事 *十七日祭（7月） *魚供養祭（8月）
== その他の神社 == その他の有川郷の神社に[[有川神社]]がある。 <gallery> 画像:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|有川神社 </gallery>
== 参考文献 == *『[[有川町]]郷土誌 平成6年』有川町郷土誌編纂委員会 *『有川の歴史の虚実』荒木文朗著 平成16年刊 *『有川二〇加』荒木文朗著 平成19年刊

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

}}

During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.

== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.

The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.

Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135505729}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.

Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

[[Category:Former Unranked Shrines in Nagasaki Prefecture]] }}

During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.

'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:17, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 03:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, [[:en:Shin-Kamigotō|Shinkamigotō]], [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]]. It is famous for its [[:en:Torii|torii]] made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and [[:en:Nagasaki Prefecture|Nagasaki Prefecture]], it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kaido-jinja (Shinkamigoto)]][[ja:海童神社 (新上五島町)]]
{{Infobox religious building| | Name = Kaitō Shrine | image = [[File:IMG_kaido.JPG|250px]] | location = {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, {{ill|Shin-Kamigotō|en|Shin-Kamigotō|WD=Q1206790|lt=Shinkamigotō}}, Arikawa-go | coordinates = {{Coord|32|59|12.2|N|129|06|51.2|E|scale:25000}} | Enshrined deity = {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}} | type = Former Unranked Shrine (旧無格社) | Founded = Unknown | Main Hall = | Other Names = | Pilgrimage Sites = | festival = | Rituals = }}
'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, {{ill|Shin-Kamigotō|en|Shin-Kamigotō|WD=Q1206790|lt=Shinkamigotō}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}. It is famous for its {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii}} made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, {{ill|Shin-Kamigotō|en|Shin-Kamigotō|WD=Q1206790|lt=Shinkamigotō|1=Shin-Kamigotō}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}. It is famous for its {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|WD=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}} made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135505729|1=Watatsumi}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|WD=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}

'''Kaitō Shrine''' (海童神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located on Ōgoshima in the Funatsu district of Arikawa-go, {{ill|Shin-Kamigotō|en|Shin-Kamigotō|qid=Q1206790|lt=Shinkamigotō|1=Shin-Kamigotō}}, {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|qid=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}. It is famous for its {{ill|Torii|en|Torii|qid=Q234731|lt=torii|1=Torii}} made from whale jawbones.
== Enshrined Deities == The main enshrined deity is {{ill|Kaitō no Kami|ja|海童神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaitō no Kami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135505729|1=Watatsumi}}, with {{ill|Hiruko|ja|蛭子神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiruko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ヒルコ|1=Hiruko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1067028}} also enshrined as an auxiliary deity.
The auxiliary deity Hiruko is the enshrined deity from the merged Funatsu Hiruko Shrine.
== History == [[File:IMG_kaido1.JPG|180px|thumb|Shrine on top of Ōgoshima]] [[File:IMG_kaido2.JPG|180px|thumb|Torii made from whale jawbones]]
The date of the shrine's founding is unknown. According to historical records from the town and {{ill|Nagasaki Prefecture|en|Nagasaki Prefecture|qid=Q169376|lt=Nagasaki Prefecture|1=Nagasaki Prefecture}}, it is said that between Genna 3 (1617) and Genna 5, drowning incidents frequently occurred among both adults and children swimming in the sea on June 17 each year in Arikawa Village. Following a divine message from Kaitō no Kami to the local official Takaira Fukuyemon, a stone shrine was built on Mikoshima to enshrine the deity.
The following year, during the festival on June 17, an improvised play called '''Niwaka Odori''' (にわかおどり) was performed, and no further drowning incidents occurred. This tradition continued annually and became the '''17th Day Festival''' (十七日祭り), a major event in Arikawa.
However, recent local historical research has identified inconsistencies in this account. Documents like the *Eguchi Manuscript*, detailing the village's fishing, agriculture, and customs from the mid-Edo period (18th century) to the early Meiji period (late 19th century), and records from {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}} suggest the shrine's origins may date back to Keichō 3 (1598). During this time, a dragon god was enshrined as a protective deity on Nakano Island in Arikawa Bay, with the island named Ōgoshima in its honor.
Additionally, historical roles like "local official" (乙名役) and the figure Takaira Fukuyemon do not appear in records until Hōreki 5 (1755), indicating they likely did not exist at the shrine's purported founding time.
Records from 1864 indicate that the custom of processions known as '''Tōrimono''' (通りもの) began on June 17, 1752, to pray for maritime safety. The form of improvised performances known as '''Niwaka''' likely originated later, influenced by theatrical trends from Osaka, Kyoto, and Edo during the late Edo period.
In the mid-Meiji period, Funatsu Hiruko Shrine was merged with Kaitō Shrine. In 1908, it was further merged with {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}, during which the worship hall and torii were dismantled, leaving only the stone shrine. A new torii was built in 1927, and in 1973, a torii made from whale bones was donated by Nittō Whaling Co., Ltd. In 2004, land reclamation projects connected Ōgoshima to the mainland.
== Festivals == ;Major Festivals and Rituals * 17th Day Festival (July) * Fish Memorial Festival (August)
== Other Shrines == Other shrines in Arikawa-go include {{ill|Arikawa Shrine|ja|有川神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arikawa Shrine|qq=}}. <gallery> File:Kami-goto.arikawa.JPG|Arikawa Shrine </gallery>
== References == * *Arikawa Town Local History* (1994), Arikawa Town Local History Compilation Committee * *The Truth and Myths of Arikawa's History*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2004 * *Arikawa Niwaka*, by Fumio Araki, published in 2007
{{Shinto-stub}}
